content.text: After a language model computes a probability distribution over its vocabulary, a decoding strategy determines which token — or sequence of tokens — is actually selected. Decoding methods range from fully deterministic algorithms that always pick the most probable token to stochastic samplers that introduce controlled randomness to produce diverse, creative, or human-like text.

At each generation step, the transformer produces a softmax distribution over all vocabulary tokens. Decoding is the algorithm applied to this distribution to select the next token. Over a full sequence, repeated token selection constitutes text decoding — the process of converting probability distributions into a final string.

Multiple distinct strategies exist, each with different quality and diversity trade-offs. Greedy decoding always selects the highest-probability token. Beam search explores multiple candidate sequences simultaneously. Sampling methods — including top-k, nucleus (top-p), and temperature scaling — introduce stochasticity, trading reproducibility for variety. Newer methods like min-p and typical sampling aim to combine the benefits of both regimes.

Understanding decoding is critical because the same model with the same prompt and context can produce dramatically different outputs depending solely on the decoding configuration. Selecting the wrong strategy for a task — greedy for creative writing, or high-temperature sampling for code generation — leads to degenerate, repetitive, or syntactically broken outputs.

Language models are probability distributions, not deterministic functions. Decoding is the bridge between that distribution and the text a user reads. The same model can appear brilliant or incoherent depending on how its probability mass is converted to tokens.

For code generation, a single syntax error can break the entire output — greedy or low-temperature decoding is almost always preferable. For creative writing, greedy decoding produces repetitive, formulaic text because the model is always drawn to the safest continuation. For summarisation, beam search typically outperforms sampling because it optimises global sequence quality rather than making locally optimal choices.

Production systems must expose decoding parameters as configuration, not hardcoded values. Different users, tasks, and contexts require different strategies; a one-size-fits-all approach consistently underperforms.
Zero-latency overhead, perfectly reproducible, excellent for deterministic tasks (code, classification, extraction).
Finds higher-quality global sequences than greedy; standard for translation, summarisation, and ASR post-processing.
A single scalar controls the creativity-coherence trade-off; easy to tune per use case.
Prevents the model from sampling very low-probability garbage tokens while preserving diversity.
Dynamically adapts the sampling vocabulary to the model's confidence level, producing more natural text.
Scales the probability threshold relative to the peak probability, making it robust across varying confidence levels.
Prone to repetition loops; may miss the globally optimal sequence by making locally suboptimal choices.
Compute and memory scale with beam width; can generate generic or dull text (the beam penalty problem); poor for open-ended generation.
High temperatures increase incoherence; must be combined with truncation methods (top-k or top-p) to avoid very low-probability tokens.
Fixed k is fragile — when the model is very confident (one token dominates), k=50 still allows 49 bad choices. When the model is uncertain, k=50 may be too restrictive.
Top-p threshold requires tuning; too high allows garbage tokens; too low collapses to near-greedy.
Less widely supported in APIs and inference frameworks; relatively new (2023).
The simplest strategy: at each step, select the token with the maximum probability.

Greedy decoding is deterministic and fast but suboptimal in two ways: it ignores the global sequence distribution (a sequence of greedy choices is not the most probable sequence), and it is susceptible to repetition loops where a common token becomes the highest-probability continuation at every step.
partial sequences (beams) simultaneously. At each step, every beam is extended by every vocabulary token, producing
is a length penalty. Without it, shorter sequences score higher because they accumulate fewer log-probability terms.
Temperature is applied before softmax by dividing the logit vector by the temperature scalar T:
, the distribution collapses to argmax (greedy). At
, the model's native distribution is used. At
, the distribution is flattened — more tokens become plausible, increasing diversity at the cost of coherence.
tokens by probability are kept; the rest are zeroed out. The remaining probability mass is redistributed and a token is sampled from it. The truncated distribution is:
The vocabulary is sorted by descending probability. The smallest set
is retained. This is more adaptive than top-k: when the model is confident, the nucleus is small (few tokens); when uncertain, it is large.
Min-p (proposed in 2023) sets a dynamic threshold proportional to the peak token's probability:

All tokens with probability below this threshold are discarded. This prevents the model from sampling near-zero tokens when confident, while still allowing broad sampling when the peak probability is low.

attrs.alt: Decoding strategies diagram: greedy, beam search, nucleus, top-k, temperature, and min-p compared
Text generation decoding methods: greedy, beam search, and sampling compared

content.text: Greedy and temperature sampling with OpenAI API

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def generate(prompt: str, temperature: float = 0.0, top_p: float = 1.0, max_tokens: int = 200) -> str:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        temperature=temperature,
        top_p=top_p,
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

# Greedy (deterministic, best for code)
code = generate("Write a Python function to reverse a string.", temperature=0.0)

# Creative (diverse, best for writing)
story = generate("Continue this story: The forest was silent except for...", temperature=1.1, top_p=0.95)
Beam search with Hugging Face Transformers

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM
import torch

# Load a summarisation model (seq2seq uses beam search natively)
model_name = "facebook/bart-large-cnn"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM.from_pretrained(model_name)

article = """
The Apollo 11 mission, launched on July 16, 1969, was the first crewed mission
to land on the Moon. Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin descended in the Lunar Module
Eagle, landing on July 20. Armstrong became the first human to walk on the Moon.
"""

inputs = tokenizer(article, return_tensors="pt", max_length=1024, truncation=True)

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model.generate(
        **inputs,
        num_beams=4,
        length_penalty=0.6,        # < 1.0 rewards shorter summaries
        no_repeat_ngram_size=3,    # prevent 3-gram repetitions
        max_length=60,
        min_length=15,
        early_stopping=True,
    )

summary = tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
print(summary)
Example 1 — Comparing decoding strategies on the same prompt

from openai import OpenAI
import time

client = OpenAI()

configs = [
    {"name": "Greedy",        "temperature": 0.0, "top_p": 1.0},
    {"name": "Low-temp",      "temperature": 0.3, "top_p": 0.9},
    {"name": "Nucleus",       "temperature": 1.0, "top_p": 0.95},
    {"name": "High-temp",     "temperature": 1.5, "top_p": 1.0},
]

prompt = "Write the opening sentence of a science fiction novel."

for cfg in configs:
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        temperature=cfg["temperature"],
        top_p=cfg["top_p"],
        max_tokens=80,
    )
    print(f"\n[{cfg['name']} T={cfg['temperature']} p={cfg['top_p']}]")
    print(resp.choices[0].message.content)
Example 2 — Min-p sampling with Hugging Face

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, MinPLogitsWarper, LogitsProcessorList
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

inputs = tokenizer("The future of artificial intelligence is", return_tensors="pt")

# MinPLogitsWarper available in transformers >= 4.42
min_p_processor = MinPLogitsWarper(min_p=0.05)

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model.generate(
        **inputs,
        do_sample=True,
        temperature=1.0,
        logits_processor=LogitsProcessorList([min_p_processor]),
        max_new_tokens=80,
        pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id,
    )
print(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
Example 3 — Custom nucleus sampler from scratch

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def nucleus_sample(logits: torch.Tensor, temperature: float = 1.0, top_p: float = 0.9) -> int:
    """Sample a token from a logit vector using nucleus (top-p) sampling."""
    logits = logits / temperature
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)

    # Sort by descending probability
    sorted_probs, sorted_indices = torch.sort(probs, descending=True)
    cumulative_probs = torch.cumsum(sorted_probs, dim=-1)

    # Remove tokens once cumulative probability exceeds top_p
    sorted_indices_to_remove = cumulative_probs - sorted_probs > top_p
    sorted_probs[sorted_indices_to_remove] = 0.0

    # Renormalise and sample
    sorted_probs = sorted_probs / sorted_probs.sum()
    sample_idx = torch.multinomial(sorted_probs, num_samples=1)
    return sorted_indices[sample_idx].item()

# Simulate logits for 5 tokens
logits = torch.tensor([3.5, 2.0, 1.5, 0.5, -2.0])
for _ in range(10):
    token = nucleus_sample(logits, temperature=1.0, top_p=0.9)
    print(f"Sampled token: {token}")

A code generation service for a developer IDE uses different decoding strategies for different completion types. Single-line completions use temperature=0 for deterministic, fast results. Multi-line function generation uses a beam search over five candidates followed by syntactic validation — only syntactically valid candidates are returned. Docstring generation uses nucleus sampling (T=0.8, top_p=0.92) to produce natural-language descriptions that are coherent but not repetitive across successive invocations.

from openai import OpenAI
import ast

client = OpenAI()

def complete_code(
    prefix: str,
    completion_type: str = "single_line",
) -> str:
    """Return a code completion using the optimal decoding strategy."""
    if completion_type == "single_line":
        # Greedy: deterministic, syntax-safe
        resp = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o",
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": "Complete the Python code. Return ONLY the completion, no explanation."},
                {"role": "user", "content": prefix},
            ],
            temperature=0,
            max_tokens=80,
        )
        return resp.choices[0].message.content

    elif completion_type == "function":
        # Generate multiple candidates, validate syntax, pick best
        candidates = []
        for _ in range(5):
            resp = client.chat.completions.create(
                model="gpt-4o",
                messages=[
                    {"role": "system", "content": "Complete the Python function body."},
                    {"role": "user", "content": prefix},
                ],
                temperature=0.4,
                top_p=0.9,
                max_tokens=300,
            )
            candidate = resp.choices[0].message.content
            try:
                ast.parse(prefix + candidate)
                candidates.append(candidate)
            except SyntaxError:
                pass
        return candidates[0] if candidates else ""

    elif completion_type == "docstring":
        # Nucleus sampling for natural variety
        resp = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o",
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": "Write a Python docstring for this function."},
                {"role": "user", "content": prefix},
            ],
            temperature=0.8,
            top_p=0.92,
            max_tokens=150,
        )
        return resp.choices[0].message.content

    return ""
Mistake 1 — Using high temperature for code without truncation

# WRONG: high temperature without top_p produces garbage tokens
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python sort function."}],
    temperature=1.5,  # Distribution is nearly flat — random garbage likely
)

# RIGHT: if creative variation needed, pair with nucleus truncation
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python sort function."}],
    temperature=0.2,  # Low for code; small variation for style
    top_p=0.9,
)
Mistake 2 — Setting top_p and temperature simultaneously to extremes

Setting both temperature=1.5 and top_p=0.5 creates conflicting signals: temperature inflates low-probability tokens while top_p then truncates them anyway. The parameters interact — start with only one at a time during tuning.
Mistake 3 — Using beam search for open-ended generation

Beam search finds the highest-probability sequences. For open-ended text, these are often generic, repetitive, and dull — the most probable continuation is often the most clichéd. Use beam search only for tasks with a well-defined correct answer (translation, summarisation, ASR, structured extraction).
Default to temperature=0 for any structured or code output; only enable sampling when diversity is explicitly needed.
For chat applications, start with temperature=1.0, top_p=1.0 (the defaults) and adjust based on user feedback.
Never use top_k and top_p simultaneously unless you understand the interaction — top_p applied after top_k can be redundant.
Use no_repeat_ngram_size=3 with beam search to prevent n-gram repetition in summarisation outputs.
Apply length penalties (length_penalty < 1.0) when shorter outputs are preferred; use > 1.0 to encourage longer outputs.
Run A/B tests with different decoding configurations on real traffic before committing to production settings.
Log the decoding parameters alongside each generation for auditability and reproducibility analysis.
Greedy decoding has a per-step cost of
for the argmax — negligible. Beam search has cost
times the KV-cache memory. At beam width 8, beam search is roughly 8× slower and 8× more memory-intensive than greedy for the decoding phase.

Nucleus and top-k sampling add a sort over the vocabulary (O(|V| log |V|)) to identify the truncation boundary, which is typically dominated by the transformer forward pass cost and is negligible in practice.

For high-throughput inference, greedy decoding and sampling are batch-parallelisable. Beam search is harder to batch because beams from different requests may terminate at different lengths. Frameworks like vLLM use paged KV-caches to reduce memory fragmentation for beam search but throughput remains lower than sampling.

# Key decoding parameters in OpenAI Chat Completions
client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[...],
    temperature=1.0,        # float [0, 2]. 0 = greedy equivalent.
    top_p=1.0,              # float (0, 1]. 1 = no truncation.
    # Note: frequency_penalty and presence_penalty modify logits
    # but are not traditional decoding parameters
    frequency_penalty=0.0,  # [-2, 2]
    presence_penalty=0.0,   # [-2, 2]
    n=1,                    # number of independent completions
    max_tokens=200,
)

from transformers import GenerationConfig

config = GenerationConfig(
    do_sample=True,          # Enable sampling (False = greedy/beam)
    temperature=0.8,
    top_p=0.92,
    top_k=50,                # 0 = disabled
    min_p=0.05,              # Min-p (newer transformers versions)
    num_beams=1,             # 1 = no beam search
    repetition_penalty=1.1, # > 1.0 discourages repeating tokens
    length_penalty=1.0,      # For beam search
    no_repeat_ngram_size=0, # 0 = disabled
    max_new_tokens=200,
)
# Save and load for reproducibility
config.save_pretrained("./my_generation_config")

Sampling strategies are non-deterministic by default. For reproducible results (essential for debugging, audits, and regression testing), use temperature=0 or seed the random number generator if your inference framework supports it. The OpenAI API does not guarantee reproducibility even at temperature=0 due to floating-point non-determinism across hardware.

Greedy decoding is especially susceptible to repetition loops: once a repeated n-gram becomes the highest-probability continuation, the model can loop indefinitely. Mitigations include no_repeat_ngram_size, repetition_penalty, and max_tokens hard limits. Always set a max_tokens limit in production to prevent infinite generation.

User-controlled temperature values can be a vector for degrading output quality or extracting information at high temperature (e.g., leaking training data memorised at specific temperature levels). Clamp temperature to a validated range before passing user-provided values to the API.

Imagine the model's vocabulary distribution as a dartboard: each sector is a token, sized proportionally to its probability. Temperature changes the size of the board: shrinking it makes the largest sector dominate (low temp = more deterministic), while expanding it makes all sectors closer in size (high temp = more random).

Top-k is a painter's tape that masks all but the k largest sectors before you throw. Top-p uses a dynamic tape that covers just enough sectors to leave a probability mass of p exposed — more sectors when the model is uncertain, fewer when confident. Min-p scales the tape relative to the biggest sector: if the top token has probability 0.7 and min_p=0.05, all tokens with probability below 0.035 are masked.

Beam search plays multiple darts simultaneously, keeps the k best scoring boards at each round, and at the end picks the board whose total dart-score is highest. It does not introduce randomness — it searches more of the deterministic space.
Decoding strategy is chosen after model training and profoundly affects output quality, diversity, and coherence.
Greedy decoding (temperature=0) is optimal for deterministic tasks like code, classification, and structured extraction.
Beam search finds the highest-probability global sequence — best for translation, summarisation, and ASR, not open-ended generation.
Nucleus sampling (top-p) dynamically adapts the sampling vocabulary to the model's confidence, producing natural, diverse text.
Temperature scales the entire probability distribution: below 1 sharpens it (less random), above 1 flattens it (more random).
Top-k and top-p can be combined, but tune them together — they interact in non-obvious ways.
Always set a max_tokens limit in production to prevent runaway generation caused by repetition loops.
Match the decoding strategy to the task: low temperature for accuracy, nucleus sampling for creativity, beam search for global quality.
